assessment_pool.title: 2024-2028 Extra Class FCC Element 4 Question Pool and Syllabus

sources.title: 2024-2028 Extra Class FCC Element 4 Question Pool and Syllabus Public Release with 4th Errata Feb 4 2026

lessons.title: Band edges, privileges, and occupied signals

questions.prompt: Why is it not legal to transmit a 3 kHz bandwidth USB signal with a carrier frequency of 14.348 MHz?

options.text: USB is not used on 20-meter phone
The lower 1 kHz of the signal is outside the 20-meter band
14.348 MHz is outside the 20-meter band
The upper 1 kHz of the signal is outside the 20-meter band

questions.explanation: A USB emission extends above its carrier. A 3 kHz signal centered at the displayed 14.348 MHz carrier occupies frequencies through about 14.351 MHz, while the 20-meter band ends at 14.350 MHz. The upper 1 kHz is therefore outside the band.

questions.prompt: When using a transceiver that displays the carrier frequency of phone signals, which of the following displayed frequencies represents the lowest frequency at which a properly adjusted LSB emission will be totally within the band?

options.text: The exact lower band edge
300 Hz above the lower band edge
1 kHz above the lower band edge
3 kHz above the lower band edge

questions.explanation: An LSB phone emission occupies frequencies below the displayed carrier. Allowing 3 kHz between the carrier and the lower edge keeps the complete properly adjusted emission inside the authorized band.

questions.prompt: What is the highest legal carrier frequency on the 20-meter band for transmitting a 2.8 kHz wide USB data signal?
questions.explanation: The 20-meter RTTY/data segment ends at 14.150 MHz. Subtracting the 2.8 kHz occupied USB width places the highest carrier at 14.1472 MHz, so the entire signal remains within the data segment.

questions.prompt: May an Extra class operator answer the CQ of a station on 3.601 MHz LSB phone?

options.text: Yes, the entire signal will be inside the SSB allocation for Extra class operators
Yes, the displayed frequency is within the 75-meter phone band segment
No, the sideband components will extend beyond the edge of the phone band segment
No, US stations are not permitted to use phone emissions below 3.610 MHz

questions.explanation: Extra-class phone privileges begin at 3.600 MHz, but an LSB emission on a 3.601 MHz displayed carrier extends below that boundary. The carrier is inside the segment while part of the sideband is not.

questions.prompt: Who must be in physical control of the station apparatus of an amateur station aboard any vessel or craft that is documented or registered in the United States?

options.text: Only a person with an FCC Marine Radio license grant
Only a person named in an amateur station license grant
Any person holding an FCC issued amateur license or who is authorized for alien reciprocal operation
Any person named in an amateur station license grant or a person holding an unrestricted Radiotelephone Operator Permit

questions.explanation: For an amateur station aboard a US-documented or registered vessel or craft, the person in physical control must hold an FCC-issued amateur operator license or be authorized for alien reciprocal operation. A marine license or radiotelephone permit is not the stated amateur-service qualification.

questions.prompt: What is the required transmit frequency of a CW signal for channelized 60 meter operation?

options.text: At the lowest frequency of the channel
At the center frequency of the channel
At the highest frequency of the channel
On any frequency where the signal’s sidebands are within the channel

questions.explanation: The current 60-meter channel rule requires a CW emission's carrier frequency to be set to the channel center frequency. The official pool's printed notation, preserved in the source-question reference, points to paragraph (h)(1); the current provision is paragraph (h)(3).

questions.prompt: What is the maximum power permitted on the 2200-meter band?

options.text: 50 watts PEP (peak envelope power)
100 watts PEP (peak envelope power)
1 watt EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power)
5 watts EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power)

questions.explanation: The 2200-meter band limit is 1 watt EIRP. The rule uses equivalent isotropic radiated power rather than transmitter PEP, so both the number and the unit matter.

questions.prompt: If a station in a message forwarding system inadvertently forwards a message that is in violation of FCC rules, who is primarily accountable for the rules violation?

options.text: The control operator of the packet bulletin board station
The control operator of the originating station
The control operators of all the stations in the system
The control operators of all the stations in the system not authenticating the source from which they accept communications

questions.explanation: When a message-forwarding station retransmits an improper communication, the control operator of the originating station is primarily accountable. Automatic forwarding does not shift primary accountability to every station in the path.

questions.prompt: Except in some parts of Alaska, what is the maximum power permitted on the 630-meter band?

options.text: 50 watts PEP (peak envelope power)
100 watts PEP (peak envelope power)
1 watt EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power)
5 watts EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power)

questions.explanation: The general 630-meter limit is 5 watts EIRP. The rule specifies a lower limit in designated parts of Alaska, which is why the question states that exception.

questions.prompt: If an amateur station is installed aboard a ship or aircraft, what condition must be met before the station is operated?

options.text: Its operation must be approved by the master of the ship or the pilot in command of the aircraft
The amateur station operator must agree not to transmit when the main radio of the ship or aircraft is in use
The amateur station must have a power supply that is completely independent of the main ship or aircraft power supply
The amateur station must operate only in specific segments of the amateur service HF and VHF bands

questions.explanation: Operation of an amateur station aboard a ship or aircraft requires approval from the ship's master or the aircraft's pilot in command. The rule does not impose the alternative equipment or band conditions listed here.

questions.prompt: What licensing is required when operating an amateur station aboard a US-registered vessel in international waters?

options.text: Any amateur license with an FCC Marine or Aircraft endorsement
Any FCC-issued amateur license
Only General class or higher amateur licenses
An unrestricted Radiotelephone Operator Permit

questions.explanation: An FCC-issued amateur license is the required amateur-service authority for operation aboard a US-registered vessel in international waters. No marine endorsement, minimum General class, or radiotelephone permit is specified.

approval.notes: Draft only. Do not change status, reviewer, or time until independent comparison and review are complete; review the E1A06 source-notation discrepancy explicitly.
